questionText: What is the correct reported speech version of the statement: 'I love eating eru and plantain.'
options: She says she loves eating eru and plantain.
She said she loves eating eru and plantain.
She said she loved eating eru and plantain.
She said she had loved eating eru and plantain.
explanation: When the reporting verb is in the past ('said'), the present simple ('love') in direct speech usually changes to past simple ('loved') in reported speech. Option C correctly applies this backshift of tense.

questionText: Change this direct question into reported speech: 'Did you see the President's speech on TV yesterday?'
options: He asked if I had seen the President's speech on TV the day before.
He asked if I saw the President's speech on TV yesterday.
He asked did I see the President's speech on TV the day before.
He asked if I have seen the President's speech on TV the previous day.
explanation: For a Yes/No question in the past simple ('Did you see'), we use 'if/whether', change the tense to past perfect ('had seen'), and update the time expression ('yesterday' becomes 'the day before'). Option A is the only one that does all three correctly.

questionText: Transform this command into reported speech: 'Clean the blackboard, John.'
options: The teacher told John clean the blackboard.
The teacher said John to clean the blackboard.
The teacher told John to clean the blackboard.
The teacher said to John that he cleans the blackboard.
explanation: Reported commands use the pattern: tell + person + (not) + to + infinitive. The verb 'said' is not used for commands. Option C correctly uses 'told John to clean'.

questionText: Which sentence correctly reports: 'Don't throw plastic bottles into the river,' the environmentalist said.
options: The environmentalist told us not to throw plastic bottles into the river.
The environmentalist told us to not throw plastic bottles into the river.
The environmentalist said us not to throw plastic bottles into the river.
The environmentalist told us didn't throw plastic bottles into the river.
explanation: For negative commands, the structure is 'tell + person + not + to + infinitive'. Option A is the standard and correct form. Option B is grammatically awkward (split infinitive) and less formal, though sometimes heard; A is the preferred taught form.

questionText: Choose the correct reported speech for: 'Where is the nearest health centre?' the man asked.
options: The man asked where the nearest health centre is.
The man asked where the nearest health centre was.
The man asked where was the nearest health centre.
The man asked where the nearest health centre has been.
explanation: For reported Wh- questions, we keep the question word ('where'), change the word order to subject + verb (no inversion), and backshift the tense ('is' becomes 'was'). Option B correctly does this: 'where the nearest health centre was'.

questionText: What changes are needed when reporting a statement from today to the next day? Direct speech: 'I will finish my homework today.'
options: He said he will finish his homework today.
He said he would finish his homework that day.
He said he would finish his homework today.
He said he finishes his homework that day.
explanation: The future simple ('will') changes to 'would', and the time expression 'today' changes to 'that day' when the time context shifts. Option B applies both changes correctly.

questionText: Report this statement about a permanent fact. Does the tense always change? 'The sun rises in the east.'
options: He said the sun rose in the east.
He said the sun rises in the east.
He said the sun has risen in the east.
He said the sun is rising in the east.
explanation: For general truths, laws of nature, or universal facts, the tense does NOT need to change in reported speech. Even if the reporting verb is in the past ('said'), we keep the present simple ('rises'). Option B is correct.

questionText: Which of these reporting verbs is best for a direct command like 'Please respect our national symbols.'?
options: asked
ordered
requested
told
explanation: The word 'Please' indicates a polite request, not a harsh command. While 'told' is neutral, 'requested' is the most accurate reporting verb for a polite command. 'Ordered' is too strong.

questionText: Report this mixed-type direct speech: 'Stop smoking,' the doctor said. 'It is bad for your lungs.'
options: The doctor told to stop smoking because it is bad for your lungs.
The doctor told me to stop smoking because it was bad for my lungs.
The doctor said stop smoking because it was bad for my lungs.
The doctor told me stop smoking because it is bad for my lungs.
explanation: The first part is a command ('Stop smoking') -> 'told me to stop'. The second part is a statement ('It is bad...') -> backshift tense ('is' to 'was') and change pronoun ('your' to 'my'). Option B does all this perfectly.

questionText: Correctly report this Yes/No question: 'Can you speak both English and French (bilingual)?'
options: She asked me if I can speak both English and French.
She asked me could I speak both English and French.
She asked me if I could speak both English and French.
She asked me that I could speak both English and French.
explanation: For a Yes/No question with a modal ('can'), we use 'if/whether', backshift the modal ('can' becomes 'could'), and keep the subject-verb order (no inversion). Option C is correct. Option A doesn't backshift. Option B has incorrect inversion.
